nodes.title: Purple Nodes
widgets_values: Conditional nodes that you may want to disable

nodes.title: Note: Variability
widgets_values:
The kSampler node, the resolution, the LoRA's, the diffusion model, the clip model, the prompt, the CFG.

The seed too, obviously, but it's one of the factors you have more control over when attempting to make changes.

The prompt is often quite sensitive to change, small changes can be made, but often even a word or two can make a big change in the results.

I want to call out the sampler node specifically, because it's not something you may have considered, but each sampler is implemented differently and introduces changes. For example, using the ClownsharKsampler will give completely different results than the Ksampler node, even with the same settings.

nodes.title: Red Nodes
widgets_values:
Important, and not generally changed: 
CLIP / VAE / Negative Prompt / FPS Switching / TripleK Strat

# The Codex
## A Library of notes collected from other workflows.

nodes.title: Note: Mystical Sources
widgets_values:
I don't know where all of these came from, but will credit whom I can:

Darksidewalker

nodes.title: Green Nodes
widgets_values: Stuff you want to change often. The "main controls", if you will. (Also notes about changes.)

nodes.title: Note: "Quality"
widgets_values:
Quality comes from steps / sampler for t2v. For i2v, you get to cheat a bit because a very high quality input image for a start frame will help improve the overall quality of the results with less steps.

Poor LoRA quality and LoRA conflicts can lower quality.

Quality can also be improved by choosing a more common aspect ratio. Wider is often sharper and more "cinematic".

nodes.title: Note: Seed Numbers
widgets_values:
There is a common misconception amongst new users: "What's a good seed number?" 

Seed numbers generally do not give you much control. 

There can be useful seeds for some circumstances: for example, two images with the very similar composition will give you similar results for the same prompt with the same seed. 

Additionally, seeds that are close numerically tend to give more similar results. (So if some tiny thing is wrong, it can be fruitful to try going up or down a tic.)

But in most scenarios, it's just randomness. So don't spend too much time testing seeds.

nodes.title: Note: Image Size
widgets_values: I created the new size node based on the info in The Codex. Now you can just pick an aspect ratio and quality. And as a quality of life improvement, if an image is enabled, it will automatically uses the image's aspect ratio (or the closest it can. You can still override!

nodes.title: Video codec, CRF and features
widgets_values:
### 🎥 H.265 (MP4) CRF Recommendations for WAN 2.2 + RIFE
---

| Purpose | CRF | Notes |
|----------|-----|-------|
| Master / Archival | 19 | Nearly lossless; best for grading or editing |
| Final Render | 20–22 | Ideal quality-to-size balance |
| Web / Social | 23–25 | Lighter files, still decent |
| Previews / Tests | 25–27 | Quick generation, visible compression |

---


💡 *Tip:* For AI-generated videos, CRF 19-21 gives extreme good results with minimal size. H265 is in most every way superior to H264 - better compression, better motion estimation, better colors.

---

nodes.title: Note: Copy/Paste Last Frame
widgets_values:
You can actually copy this to be the first frame or the last frame. Want to make a longer video? You can use this trick to do it: 

- Right click the preview, select 'Copy (Clipspace)'. 

- You can then right click on either Start Image Loader or End Image Loader and right click to 'Paste (Clipspace)'. 

Unfortunately, this technique has lots of problems, I talk about on the workflow description page on CivitAI.

nodes.title: Note: Model Precision
widgets_values:
| Format / Mode | Bit Width / Structure | Rank (Higher → Lower Precision) | Advantages / When Good | Disadvantages / Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Base Precision Formats** | | | | |
| FP32 | 32-bit float | **1 (highest)** | Very high accuracy; large dynamic range; minimal numerical issues. | High VRAM, slower compute. |
| BF16 | 16-bit float (same exponent bits as FP32, fewer mantissa) | **2** | Close dynamic range to FP32; good trade-off of precision vs memory. | Less fraction precision; possible rounding errors. |
| FP16 | 16-bit float (IEEE half precision) | **3** | Much lower memory; good speed; widely supported. | Less dynamic range; more risk of underflow/overflow; less precision. |
| **Quantization / FP8 / Lower Precision Variants** | | | | |
| FP8 E4M3 (scaled, non-fast / "fp8_e4m3fn_scaled") | 8-bit float: 1 sign, 4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa | **4** | Better precision for small values; useful detail close to zero; decent dynamic range. | Lower dynamic range; need calibration / scaling; possibly slower if fallback. |
| FP8 E5M2 (scaled) | 8-bit float: 1 sign, 5 exponent bits, 2 mantissa | **5** | More dynamic range (can handle bigger/smaller extremes) with some calibration; less error in wide distribution. | Less precision in mantissa; subtle detail around small values may be lost. |
| FP8 E4M3 / E5M2 (fast / non-scaled) | Same structures but with fewer calibrations / optimizations | **6** | Faster; less memory / compute overhead; may still be usable. | More quantization error; possible artifacts; more numerical instability. |
| Q8 / Int8 (integer quantization) | 8-bit integer (no floating exponent) | **7 (lowest)** | Very compact; often fastest; works well if perfect fidelity not required. | Significant loss of precision; limited dynamic range; visible errors in many cases. |

nodes.title: Note: Film Grain
widgets_values: A little film grain improves the general impression of video by adding subtle motion.

nodes.title: Note: Clean VRAM / Cache
widgets_values: If you start hitting VRAM errors during long sessions, consider un-bypassing these.

nodes.title: Note: Color Correction
widgets_values: LoRA's and other settings can cause desaturation, this "Color Correct" node provides a bit of extra 'pop'. Bypass this if things look accurate.

nodes.title: Note: LTX 2.3 I2V
widgets_values: The default nodes for LTX 2.3 work differently than Wan, so there is a basic boolean value to control this. The image connection and nodes do not bypass well. We just rely on this toggle, which is less than ideal.

nodes.title: Unmute to Override Image Size (try x32's)
Note: Pinning
widgets_values:
press p: 
Will toggle for selected node. The red pins are used to keep stuff from getting dragged or expanded with the button on the top left.

nodes.title: Note: Length
widgets_values: LTX 2.3 will approach 20 seconds. Jury is out on if it you get better resuls with shorter videos.

nodes.title: Note: Update LoRA's
widgets_values:
press r: 
You can update the the LoRA's and models available without browser refreshing ComfyUI.

nodes.title: LTX 2.3 Prompting Guidance
widgets_values:
LTX Video 2.3 tends to respond best to **longer, descriptive prompts** that clearly explain the shot, subject, action, camera, and sound. The official guidance recommends writing the prompt as **one flowing paragraph**, using **present tense**, and aiming for about **4–8 descriptive sentences**.

## The basic formula

A good LTX prompt usually works best in this order: 
**shot → scene → action → character → camera → audio**. 
That structure matches the official prompting guide. 

### 1. Establish the shot

Start by saying what kind of shot this is and what style you want.

Examples:

* close-up
* medium shot
* wide shot
* overhead shot
* handheld documentary look
* cinematic fantasy look
* noir style

The official guide recommends using cinematography terms and shot scale early in the prompt.

### 2. Set the scene

Describe the environment and mood.

Include things like:

* location
* time of day
* lighting
* color palette
* atmosphere
* surface texture

Examples:

* neon-lit alley at night
* warm golden sunset light
* fog drifting through pine trees
* glossy wet pavement
* muted blue-gray palette

LTX’s docs specifically call out lighting, color palette, textures, and atmosphere as important scene ingredients.

### 3. Describe the action

Say what is happening in clear sequence from start to finish.

Use present-tense motion language such as:

* walks
* turns
* reaches
* looks up
* runs
* drifts
* smiles
* raises a hand

The official guide recommends present tense and a natural action sequence.

### 4. Define the character

Add the details that matter visually.

Useful details:

* approximate age
* hair
* clothing
* silhouette
* facial expression
* distinguishing features

The docs recommend expressing emotion through visible physical cues instead of vague labels alone.

### 5. Describe the camera

Explain how the camera moves **relative to the subject**.

Examples:

* slow dolly in toward the subject
* low-angle tracking shot
* handheld follow from behind
* gentle pan left as the subject turns
* static camera with shallow depth of field

LTX’s docs specifically recommend describing camera movement relative to the subject, and recent API updates also added explicit camera motion options like `dolly_in`, `dolly_out`, `dolly_left`, `dolly_right`, `jib_up`, `jib_down`, `static`, and `focus_shift`.

### 6. Add audio

If audio matters, describe it clearly.

Examples:

* distant traffic hum
* soft wind in the trees
* footsteps on wet concrete
* quiet piano music
* spoken dialogue in quotation marks

The official guide says to describe ambient sound, music, speech, or singing, and to put spoken dialogue in quotation marks.


---

## How much detail to use

LTX’s official guidance says **close-ups need more detail than wide shots**, and that longer descriptive prompts usually improve **motion, coherence, and audio alignment**.

A simple rule:

* **Wide shot:** focus more on environment, atmosphere, and broad movement.
* **Medium shot:** balance character, action, and camera language.
* **Close-up:** add more facial, texture, and micro-action detail.

---

## Good prompt habits

* Write in **one flowing paragraph** instead of comma-stuffed keywords.
* Use **present tense** for motion.
* Aim for about **4–8 descriptive sentences**.
* Be specific about **camera movement** and what the subject is doing during that movement.
* Include **audio** when it matters to the shot.
* Iterate quickly; LTX’s docs explicitly encourage experimentation.

---

## What to avoid

These are not hard bans, but they usually make prompts weaker:

* Very short prompts with only a subject and no scene or motion.
* Long unordered keyword piles with no clear sequence.
* Vague actions like “does something cool.”
* Camera language with no relationship to the subject.
* Overdescribing tiny details in a very wide shot.

These are practical takeaways from the official guidance on flow, shot scale, and motion clarity.

nodes.title: Width and height from aspect ratio 🪴
Note: 🎞️ Interpolators
widgets_values: LTX 2.3 allow you to specify the framerate! It produces weird results with interpolation because the final result is full framerate by default. It produces slow motion and the audio falls out of sync.

nodes.title: Note: Last Frame?
widgets_values: Last frame images will return later. The official nodes don't support it, but I'm exploring some alternative techniques.

nodes.title: Note: Sampler Performance
widgets_values: If you'd like even faster performance, change this to "euler". I've chosen to leave "euler_ancestral_cfg_pp" as the default as the quality is higher, but still more performant than the "Advanced" process.

nodes.title: Note: Upscale Model
widgets_values: There are a bunch of versions of these upscale LoRA's, but we'll stick with the spatial version for now. But feel free to explore the others!

nodes.title: Version Notes:
widgets_values:
# Yet Another Workflow
### v 0.39 (LTX 2.3) - [Runpod Setup Guide and Install](https://civitai.com/articles/26397/yet-another-workflow-for-wan-22-step-by-step-with-runpod-template-v038b)
- Initial adaptation for LTX 2.3 including the full sweet of official sampler options and changes to facilitate their different nodes
- Created a new image size custom node to reduce size adjustments for i2v
- Switching node isolate to improve install issues

nodes.title: Note: Image Sharpen
widgets_values: I find a little sharpening is nice, but i2v can end up over-sharp if the input image was very sharp to begin with. Consider bypassing.

nodes.title: Note: Sharpening Issue
widgets_values: 10 second high res videos break the math of that node, as such I am leaving it bypassed by default. It will work fine otherwise. Considering alternatives.

nodes.title: Note: Sampler Stages
widgets_values: Unlike with Wan, the output of the 1st Sampler will look decent, but with less detail. Enable the 2nd Sampler to upscale and do refinement. (Be careful about resolution, 2nd pass doubles, so start smaller!) The Advanced Sampler is a different technique that is much slower.
